text: The surgeon observes small bowel adhesions to the colon and pelvis, with the appendix drawn into the area. The appendix is separated from the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon and uterus, and an appendectomy is performed using a stapler. The surgeon notes the presence of the right ovary and fallopian tube obscuring the view of the uterus and bladder, and plans to reposition these structures.

The surgeon encounters small bowel adhesions to the colon and pelvis, with the appendix drawn into the area. The appendix is separated from the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon and uterus, and an appendectomy is performed using a stapler. The surgeon then observes a large ovary and fallopian tube obscuring the view of the uterus and bladder, noting that the right ovary is positioned over the left side.

The surgeon clears adhesions involving the right ovary, which is adherent to the pericolic fat and mesentery of the sigmoid colon. The ovary is also slightly adherent to the bladder, with the uterus visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon clears adhesions between the right ovary and the pericolic fat and mesentery of the sigmoid colon. The ovary is also adherent to the bladder, and the surgeon carefully separates these attachments, repositioning the ovary to the correct side.

The surgeon identifies the right ovary and removes its adhesions to the bladder, freeing up the uterus. A Keith needle is inserted to retract the uterus, facilitating the mobilization of the colon. The assist port with air seal is visible, aiding in the procedure. The surgeon continues to mobilize the colon from the back of the uterus, approaching the cavity where the abscess is anticipated.

The surgeon uses a Keith needle to retract the uterus, freeing up one of the arms for better access. An assist port with air seal is in place. The surgeon mobilizes the colon, detaching it from the back of the uterus, while anticipating the appearance of the abscess cavity.

The surgeon examines the deep pelvic collection located on the left side, posterior to the cervix and uterus. Chronic inflammatory signs are visible on the pelvic side wall. The surgeon attempts to navigate through the abscess cavity, initially without success, and then tries accessing it from the right side.

The surgeon examines the deep pelvic collection located on the left side, posterior to the cervix and behind the uterus. Chronic inflammatory signs are visible on the pelvic side wall. The surgeon attempts to navigate through the abscess cavity but encounters difficulty, prompting exploration on the right side.

The surgeon uses suction as a retractor to manage oozing, packing the area with a sponge. The colon is mobilized for anastomosis, including the splenic flexure, visible with the spleen in the corner. The ureter is used as a lateral boundary to enter the pelvis, with Firefly mode aiding in identifying the ureter's location to avoid injury during dissection.

The surgeon uses suction as a retractor to manage oozing, packing the area with a sponge. The colon is mobilized for anastomosis, including the splenic flexure, without needing to adjust the Trendelenburg position.

The surgeon identifies the spleen in the upper corner of the surgical field. They proceed to enter the pelvis using the left ureter as a lateral boundary, following it down into the left pelvis. The Firefly mode is activated to visualize the ureters, facilitating a quicker dissection without the need to repeatedly locate the ureter manually.

The surgeon navigates the left side wall, encountering adnexal tissue fused to the colon. The GYN colleague identifies and divides the left gonadal vessels using bipolar cautery, aided by Firefly imaging to locate the ureter in the bloody field. The dissection proceeds into the retroperitoneum behind the gonadal vessels, utilizing the fallopian tube dissection to access the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon navigates down the left side wall, encountering adnexal tissue fused to the colon. The GYN colleague identifies and divides the left gonadal vessels using bipolar cautery, aided by Firefly imaging to clearly visualize the ureter in the bloody field. The dissection proceeds into the retroperitoneum behind the gonadal vessels, with efforts to re-enter the pelvic cavity by dissecting the fallopian tube.

The surgeon dissects the left fallopian tube at the junction with the round ligament, visible in the surgical field. The left pelvic peritoneum is being dissected to transition from the retroperitoneum back into the pelvic plane. The surgeon removes the remaining attachments of the colon from the back of the uterus and cervix, revealing the deep pelvis.

The surgeon dissects the left fallopian tube at the junction with the round ligament, visible in the surgical field. The left pelvic peritoneum is being dissected to transition from the retroperitoneum back into the pelvic plane. The surgeon examines the deep pelvis and continues to detach the colon from the posterior aspect of the uterus and cervix.

The surgeon has successfully separated the left-sided attachments and is examining the deep pelvis, which appears healthy. The suction irrigator is used as an instrument to assist in visualization. The GYN colleague completes the right salpingo-oophorectomy, leaving the uterus in place due to minimal involvement in the disease. The surgeon proceeds with the bowel resection, preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery and taking down the mesentery to the rectus sigmoid junction. The proximal and distal bowel appear healthy, and a drain is placed despite no active feculent or purulent collection.

The surgeon has successfully separated the structures on the left-hand side, revealing a clear view of the deep pelvis. The suction irrigator is used to assist in visualizing and clearing the area. The surgeon notes the absence of any fistula to the back of the vagina and prepares to proceed with the anastomosis.

The surgeon allows the GYN colleague to complete the right salpingo-oophorectomy, leaving the uterus in place due to minimal involvement in the disease. The surgeon then proceeds with the bowel resection, preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery while taking down the proximal mesentery to the rectosigmoid junction. The visual field shows healthy proximal and distal bowel with a chronic pelvic collection but no active feculents or purulence.

The surgeon performs a proximal division of the bowel, opting for an intracorporeal anastomosis. The specimen is moved to the right side, and the surgeon begins a sewn anastomosis by aligning the posterior staple lines and creating a colotomy and proctotomy to join the openings. A fan and steel incision is planned for specimen extraction.

The surgeon performs a proximal division of the bowel and prepares for an intracorporeal anastomosis. The specimen is moved to the right side, and the surgeon begins a sewn anastomosis by aligning the two posterior staple lines, creating a colotomy and proctotomy, and joining the openings. A fan and steel incision is planned for specimen extraction.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis by sewing the inner layer, starting on the left side and then moving to the right, creating a wide open connection between the bowel segments. Large collotomy and proctotomy openings are made, and the mucosa is carefully dunked to prevent bulging. Seromuscular bites are used to imbricate the staple line, and a leak test is performed using a rigid proctoscope. The specimen is extracted through a Pfannenstiel incision, and a pelvic drain is left in place.

The surgeon performs a wide open anastomosis, creating large collotomy and proctotomy openings. Absorbable sutures are used to close the openings, ensuring no bulging mucosa is visible. Seromuscular bites are taken to oppose the staple line, followed by tying to the original stay suture. A leak test is conducted using a rigid proctoscope, and a pelvic drain is left in place. The specimen is extracted through a fan and steel incision.
